explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count chatter enabled, generating extra messages for every statement.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF is used while the procedure issues transactions, allowing runtime errors to leave transactions partially applied.
SELECT * exposes every column of Sales.Orders and prevents covering-index benefits.
SELECT TOP (10) o.OrderID FROM Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the chosen rows are arbitrary.
DISTINCT on Sales.Orders.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) wraps the OrderDate column and prevents a simple range seek.
o.CustomerID (int) is compared to @CustomerIdText (nvarchar), forcing an implicit type conversion.
The @CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID pattern blocks seeks and accurate cardinality estimates.
LIKE N'%urgent%' on Sales.Orders.Comments has a leading wildcard that prevents an index seek.
NOT IN (SELECT i.CustomerID FROM Sales.Invoices AS i) yields surprising results if the subquery ever produces NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the CustomerID column and makes the predicate non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID performs arithmetic on the OrderID column, preventing direct index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 typically forces a scan of Sales.Orders instead of a seek.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers multiplies rows without a join predicate.
SELECT TOP (1) in the CROSS JOIN query has no ORDER BY, so the chosen row is arbitrary.
Comma-separated Sales.Orders AS o, Sales.OrderLines AS ol with the join condition buried in WHERE uses the legacy join style.
SELECT TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the chosen row is arbitrary.
The scalar (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Sales.OrderLines AS ol WHERE ol.OrderID = o.OrderID) executes once per outer row.
Correlated EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Sales.OrderLines AS ol WHERE ol.OrderID = o.OrderID AND ol.Quantity > 1) should be expressed as a set-based join.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END expression is duplicated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list column position and breaks when the SELECT list changes.
OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY with only ORDER BY o.OrderDate has no stable tie breaker for duplicate dates.
UNION of WHERE o.OrderID = 1 and WHERE o.OrderID = 2 performs needless duplicate removal between disjoint literal keys; use UNION ALL.
(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Sales.OrderLines AS ol WHERE ol.OrderID = o.OrderID) > 0 counts all matching rows when EXISTS would suffice.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID FROM Sales.Orders silently keeps the value from the last row processed and masks multi-row results.
Table variable @OrderIds has no statistics and can yield poor cardinality estimates on older compatibility levels.
SELECT TOP (5) o.OrderID inserted into @OrderIds has no ORDER BY, so the chosen rows are arbitrary.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp-table schema implicitly; declare it explicitly to control columns and indexing.
SELECT TOP (5) into #ImplicitOrders has no ORDER BY, so the selected rows are arbitrary.
DECLARE order_cursor CURSOR begins a row-by-row iteration that should be expressed as a set-based statement.
SELECT TOP (2) inside the cursor declaration has no ORDER BY, so the rows fed into the cursor are arbitrary.
WHILE @Counter <= 1 processes work iteratively instead of as a set-based statement.
SET @Sql builds SQL by concatenating @CustomerID into the string.
User-supplied @CustomerID is concatenated directly into a dynamic SQL string executed by EXEC, exposing the procedure to SQL injection.
EXEC (@Sql) executes a string with no parameterized inputs.
SET @Sql builds SQL by concatenating @OrderID into the string.
EXEC sys.sp_executesql @Sql uses sp_executesql but still embeds the value rather than binding it as a parameter.
CREATE TABLE #Scratch is never dropped before the procedure ends, leaving the temp object until scope cleanup.
The CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor signals failure to the caller.
RAISERROR is the legacy error-raising mechanism; use THROW for new error paths.
PRINT is message-buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
o.CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly in the query.
o.OrderDate >= '20160101' embeds an unlabelled date constant in query logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') relies on session-sensitive date string conversion without an explicit style.
COALESCE(o.CustomerID, 0) = COALESCE(i.CustomerID, 0) in the ON clause inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the chosen row is arbitrary.
Self-joining Sales.Orders to Sales.Orders on the same OrderID retrieves information already available in a single row.
SELECT TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the chosen row is arbitrary.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts by an nvarchar(MAX) text column that is unselective and expensive to sort.
SELECT SUM(ol.Quantity) FROM Sales.OrderLines aggregates the entire fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate and belongs in WHERE, not HAVING.
ORDER BY OrderCount on a single-row aggregate result is unnecessary.
MERGE on Sales.Orders has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; prefer separate UPDATE/INSERT blocks.
target.Comments = target.Comments inside the MERGE writes no actual change but still triggers logging and locking.
UPDATE Sales.Orders SET Comments = Comments writes no actual change but still acquires locks and generates log records.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines WHERE Quantity = -1 uses a non-key business value as the predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread; use asynchronous scheduling instead.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is issued without a BEGIN TRY / BEGIN CATCH wrapper, leaving no error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session state and couples the procedure to identity management.
EXEC sp_who calls a system routine without a schema qualifier; use sys.sp_who.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 is changed and never restored, affecting the rest of the session.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits the explicit column list, breaking if the temp-table schema changes.
WHERE CustomerName LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' has a leading wildcard that prevents an index seek on Sales.Customers.CustomerName.
SET @sql builds SQL by concatenating @CustomerName into the string.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into a dynamic SQL string executed by sp_executesql, exposing the procedure to SQL injection.
EXEC sp_executesql @sql uses sp_executesql but embeds @CustomerName as a literal instead of binding it as a parameter.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers SET CustomerCategoryID = 1 has no WHERE clause and will rewrite every row.
WHERE o.OrderDate >= '2016-01-01' is placed on the outer-joined Sales.Orders table, effectively converting the LEFT JOIN into an inner join.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL is always unknown because NULL cannot equal anything; use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) is a scalar UDF called per row in the SELECT list and forces row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block around BEGIN TRANSACTION ... COMMIT does not issue ROLLBACK, leaving the transaction open on error.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes an index hint on Sales.Customers, coupling the query to a specific physical index.
The query nests four levels of derived tables (t1, t2, t3, outer SELECT), which should be refactored into CTEs or a single set-based query.
WHERE CustomerName LIKE '%A%' has a leading wildcard that prevents an index seek on Sales.Customers.CustomerName.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti exposes every column of the TVF and prevents column-level optimization.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would let the optimizer integrate its logic into the calling query.
